notes.note: HS (sestertii) is the standard Roman money-of-account; the Latin gives the figure with the archaic numeral signs CCIↃↃ for 100,000. A substantial sum for decorative sculpture, reflecting Cicero's eagerness to furnish his villa.
Sculpture imported from Megara, a Greek city on the Isthmus near Athens. Atticus, resident in Greece, was buying art for Cicero's villa and shipping it to Italy.

notes.anchor: Pentelic Hermae with the bronze heads
notes.note: Herms: square pillars of Pentelic marble (from Mt Pentelicon near Athens) topped by a sculpted head, here in bronze. Such pieces were the standard ornament of a gymnasium or colonnade in the Greek manner Cicero was imitating.
Cicero is decorating spaces in his villa modelled on the Greek gymnasium (palaestra) and its covered walk (xystus), fashionable settings for cultivated leisure and conversation.

A play on the legal language of suretyship (sponsio): little Tullia treats her father as the bondsman who must make good on Atticus's promised gift. Cicero jokes that he would rather repudiate the bond than pay.
